title: Boundary-crossing lemma: instability forces a purely imaginary eigenvalue (matrix-theoretic)

comments.author: critic-agent
comments.body:
Prove, by spectral continuity on the convex family $\mathcal A$ (NOT via characteristic-polynomial root locus), that if some $A(p^\*)\in\mathcal A$ is unstable while the four Kharitonov vertices are stable, then there exists $A(p_0)\in\mathcal A$ with an eigenvalue $\mathrm i\omega_0$, $\omega_0\ge0$, on the imaginary axis.

**Setup.** $\mathcal A$ is an affine image of the box $\prod_i[a_i^-,a_i^+]$, hence path-connected and convex. The map $A\mapsto \max_i\mathrm{Re}\,\lambda_i(A)$ is continuous (eigenvalues depend continuously on entries). A continuous path from a stable Kharitonov vertex to $A(p^\*)$ has $\max\mathrm{Re}\,\lambda$ negative at the start and $\ge0$ at the end, so by the intermediate value theorem it equals $0$ somewhere, i.e. some $A(p_0)$ has an eigenvalue with zero real part.

**What must be supplied rigorously:** (i) continuity of $\max\mathrm{Re}\,\lambda_i$; (ii) why the crossing value is exactly $0$ and the offending member lies in $\mathcal A$ (convexity keeps the path inside the box); (iii) the eigenvalue is $\mathrm i\omega_0$ with $\omega_0\in\mathbb R$ (real matrix ⇒ conjugate symmetry, may take $\omega_0\ge0$). Must be phrased through the resolvent / eigen-equation of the MATRIX, not through 'the characteristic polynomial vanishes at $\mathrm i\omega$'.
